author: praasz
title: JS API E2E Electron test cleanup fails with Permission Denied on Windows
signature: rm cannot remove electron.exe Permission denied|Flaky Test

failed_jobs.name: JS API (26)
failed_jobs.symptom: after all hook fails: rm -rf demo-electron-app-project returns Permission denied on Electron dist files

key_errors:
Error: Command failed: rm -rf demo-electron-app-project
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/d3dcompiler_47.dll': Permission denied
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/electron.exe': Permission denied
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/libEGL.dll': Permission denied
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/libGLESv2.dll': Permission denied
root_cause: The Electron E2E test runs on Windows and spawns an Electron process. After test completion, the after-all cleanup hook attempts to delete the demo project directory using 'rm -rf'. However, the Electron process (or Windows file handles) keeps locks on electron.exe and its DLLs, preventing deletion. The 2 actual tests pass; only the cleanup hook fails. This is a Windows file-locking issue with Electron binaries and is not related to the PR changes.
reproduction_steps: Run 'npm run test:e2e' on a Windows runner after the Electron app test, and observe the after-all cleanup failing to remove locked Electron DLLs.
recommended_actions: Fix the E2E cleanup hook to handle Windows file locks: use 'rimraf' or 'del' npm packages that handle Windows file locking, or use a Windows-native rmdir command.
Alternatively, ensure the Electron process is fully terminated before running rm, e.g. using taskkill on Windows.
Re-run the failed jobs as a short-term workaround - the failure is transient.
prevention_strategies: Use platform-aware cleanup utilities (e.g., rimraf) in test after-all hooks that run on Windows runners to handle locked Electron binary files.
historical_context: This exact failure pattern (Electron dist files Permission denied on Windows during cleanup) was previously seen on 2026-07-09 (run 29010970383, PR #36780) and 2026-07-07 (run 28861100887, PR #36744). It is a recurring infrastructure/flaky issue unrelated to PR content.
